MOTU UltraLite-mk3

Section-based notes for routing, CueMix, converters, monitoring, and hardware setup.

  • Overview
  • Hybrid FireWire/USB2
  • Mic/Guitar Inputs
  • Comprehensive I/O
  • Mixing and effects
  • CueMix FX software
  • Classic reverb
  • Modeled analog EQ
  • Vintage compression
  • Tuner & analysis tools
  • Clock and sync
  • MIDI
  • Expansion & drivers
  • Feature Summary

Vintage Compression

Source: MOTU product page

The UltraLite-mk3 provides two types of compression in CueMix FX, both running in hardware DSP with zero impact on the computer's CPU.

Conventional Compressor

A standard compressor with manual parameters:

  • Threshold: the level at which compression begins. Set lower for more compression; higher for only peak control.
  • Ratio: how much the signal is reduced once it crosses the threshold. 2:1 for gentle levelling; 4:1–8:1 for more aggressive control; 10:1+ for limiting.
  • Attack: how quickly the compressor responds after the signal crosses threshold. Slower attack preserves the initial transient (punch); faster attack controls it immediately.
  • Release: how quickly the compressor stops compressing after the signal drops below threshold.
  • Makeup Gain: compensates for the gain reduction — bring the compressed signal back up to the original perceived level.

Leveler™ (LA-2A Model)

An accurate model of the legendary Teletronix LA-2A optical compressor — one of the most musical vocal compressors ever made:

  • Program-dependent response: the attack and release times are not fixed — they react dynamically to the nature of the audio program material. Loud transients compress faster; quieter sustained notes release more naturally. This is what makes it sound "invisible".
  • Peak Reduction: the primary control. Turn clockwise to increase compression depth. Start at 30–40% for transparent gain riding on the SM58 vocal; increase to 50–60% for more visible levelling on dynamic vocalists.
  • Gain: makeup gain to restore output level after compression.

Recommended Settings

  • SM58 vocals: use the Leveler (LA-2A). Set Peak Reduction to 35–45% for transparent dynamic control. Let the optical response handle timing automatically — no attack/release adjustment needed.
  • Hydrasynth bus: use the Conventional Compressor with high threshold (−6 dBFS), 2:1 ratio, fast attack (5–10 ms), medium release (100–200 ms). Catches only the loudest peaks without affecting the dynamic expression of the patches.
  • General limiting: Conventional Compressor at 10:1 ratio, −3 dBFS threshold, fast attack — prevents clipping on unexpected loud sources without affecting the overall sound.

Built for fast lookups, cleaner routing decisions, and practical studio recall.
